meta.title: F06 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: BMW 6 Series F06 engine replacement costs — N55, N57, N63, S63 petrol and diesel. Real UK prices, V8 ownership costs, M6 maintenance, and Gran Coupé‑specific guidance from 20+ vetted BMW specialists.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 6 Series F06 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned F06 engine typically costs £3,500–£14,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the N57 in the 640d sits at £4,500–£7,000, the N55 in the 640i is £3,500–£6,500, the N63 in the 650i is £6,000–£10,000, and the S63 in the M6 is £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F06 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N55B30A in the 640i (2011–2018) is the most dependable petrol engine in the F06 range — BMW's single-turbo straight-six is robust, chain-driven, and free of the N63's hot-vee issues. On the diesel side, the N57D30B in the 640d is the most reliable option — 313 PS, proven reliability, and accessible chain. The N63 V8 in the 650i and S63 in the M6 are the least reliable and most expensive to maintain.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 6 Series F06?

acceptedAnswer.text: Valve stem seal failure on the N63 in the 650i, turbocharger and valve stem wear on the S63 in the M6, and timing chain wear on the N57 in the 640d are the main engine issues. Air suspension compressor failure is the most common non-engine issue. The F06's 4+1 seating and Gran Coupé body don't affect engine reliability but can make some repairs more complex.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 6 Series F06 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends heavily on which engine you choose. The N55 straight-six in the 640i and N57 straight-six diesel in the 640d are well-regarded and proven. The N63 V8 in the 650i carries a significant risk of valve stem seal failure — budget for this repair. The S63 in the M6 is the most complex and expensive to maintain. As with any BMW, service history is everything.

mainEntity.name: Which BMW 6 Series F06 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: We don't say 'avoid' outright — but an N63-powered 650i without valve stem seal history is a significant gamble. An S63-powered M6 without documented maintenance history is even more of a risk. A well-maintained N55 640i or N57 640d is the safest choice. If you must have a V8, budget £3,000–£6,000 for valve stem seals at 50,000–80,000 miles.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my F06 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Blue smoke on startup (N63/S63 valve stem seals), excessive oil consumption (N63/S63), loss of power or turbo whine (S63), or timing chain rattle (N57) are the key warning signs. On the 650i, oil consumption of more than 1 litre per 1,000 miles is a classic N63 symptom. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 6 Series F06 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £20,000 F06, a £4,500–£7,000 N57 recon is 22–35% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £10,000 example, the same recon is 45–70% — that's at or above our 70% BMW threshold, so we'd advise walking away. For an N63 650i or S63 M6, the economics are even less favourable unless the car is low-mileage and well-specified. Always value the car first.

mainEntity.name: How long do BMW 6 Series F06 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The N57 diesel six can exceed 200,000 miles with proper servicing — it's one of BMW's most durable engines. The N55 petrol six is similarly robust. The N63 V8 is the least durable — valve stem seal failure is common before 100,000 miles. The S63 in the M6 requires rigorous maintenance; with proper care, it can reach 150,000+ miles, but running costs are significant.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the F06, F12, and F13?

acceptedAnswer.text: The F12 is the 6 Series convertible (2011–2018), the F13 is the 6 Series coupé (2011–2018), and the F06 is the 6 Series Gran Coupé (2011–2018). All three share the same platform and engine range. The F06 is the four-door fastback variant with a 4+1 seating arrangement, a wheelbase 4.6 inches longer than the F13, and a more practical rear cabin. This page covers the F06 specifically — see the F12 and F13 pages for convertible and coupé variants.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my BMW 6 Series F06 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block. Common F06 codes include N55B30A (640i), N63B44A (650i pre-LCI), N63B44B (650i LCI), N63B40A (650i 4.0L, some markets), N57D30B (640d), and S63B44B (M6). Tell us your variant and registration date and we'll identify it for you.

hero.h1: BMW 6 Series F06 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the 6 Series Gran Coupé F06 — from the 3.0-litre N55 straight-six in the 640i, to the 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 in the 650i, the 313 bhp N57 diesel in the 640d, and the fire-breathing S63 V8 in the M6. Real UK costs, honest failure data, and the 2015 facelift that kept BMW's most elegant four-door coupé relevant. Backed by 120+ real UK F06 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 6 Series F06 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the F06 Gran Coupé — from the entry-level 640i to the range-topping M6, including the 640d diesel that was specially built for European customers.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: The F06 uses BMW's established engine families — N55 (six-cylinder petrol), N57 (six-cylinder diesel), N63 (V8 petrol), and S63 (M V8). The N55 is widely regarded as a dependable engine. The N63 V8 has a well-earned reputation for high ownership costs due to its hot-vee design. The S63 in the M6 is the most powerful and most expensive to maintain. All ratings are [PROVISIONAL] pending dedicated Engine Pages and verified enquiry data for this specific model.

overview.intro: The BMW 6 Series Gran Coupé F06 is the four-door fastback variant of the third-generation 6 Series, launched in June 2012 at the Geneva International Motor Show. Designed by Nader Faghihzadeh, the F06 shares its platform with the F10 5 Series and features a wheelbase 4.6 inches longer than the F13 coupé, with a 4+1 seating arrangement. The engine range includes the N55 straight-six petrol in the 640i, the N63 V8 in the 650i, the N57 straight-six diesel in the 640d, and the S63 V8 in the M6. A 2015 facelift brought subtle styling updates while the engine line-up remained unchanged. Production ended in 2018, succeeded by the 8 Series Gran Coupé.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW L6 (shared with F10 5 Series) • Predecessor: BMW 6 Series E63 • Successor: BMW 8 Series G15 • Sibling body styles in this generation: F12 Convertible, F13 Coupé.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The BMW 6 Series F06 generated 120 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [EM-VERIFIED], concentrated on the N63 V8s and N57 diesels as these cars age out of warranty.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre straight-six diesel produces 313 PS and 630 Nm — effortless, refined, and with the kind of torque that makes the F06 a genuine long-distance cruiser. The N57 is chain-driven and proven across the BMW range. In the Gran Coupé, it's the diesel you buy if you want six-cylinder refinement without V8 running costs.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want effortless torque and 40+ mpg potential.

items.quote: The N55 is BMW's single-turbo straight-six that replaced the N54 — 320 PS, twin-scroll turbo, and significantly more reliable than its predecessor. The F06 was one of the last models to receive the N55 before the B58 took over. It's the petrol you can buy without HPFP anxiety.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder petrol performance without the N63's V8 running costs. (Model-wide: B58B30 in the G30/G32 sets the modern benchmark — see G30 →)

items.quote: The hot-vee V8 suffers from heat-related failures — valve stem seals, turbo coolant lines, and battery drain are common. The N63 has a well-earned reputation for high ownership costs. The 650i is fast, but it's expensive to keep fast.
items.whoItsFor: buyers with deep pockets who want V8 performance and are prepared for the maintenance bills.

items.quote: The S63 is the high-performance version of the N63 — 560–575 PS, twin-turbo, and even more complex. Valve stem seal failure, turbocharger wear, and the 7-speed DCT transmission all add to the ownership cost. A full engine replacement sits at £8,000–£14,000.
items.whoItsFor: M6 buyers who budget for high-end maintenance and have a specialist they trust.

items.quote: The 640d offers 313 PS, 630 Nm, and 40+ mpg potential — it's the most efficient engine in the range and the cheapest to replace of the six-cylinder options. The N57 is robust, the chain is accessible, and the F06's weight doesn't overwhelm it.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best balance of performance, economy, and reliability.

items.quote: The 640i is often overshadowed by the V8 650i and the M6, but the N55 straight-six is the most dependable petrol engine in the range. 320 PS and 450 Nm is more than enough for the F06's grand touring mission, and running costs are significantly lower than the V8s.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the V8's running costs.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals / turbo coolant lines
rows.ownershipVerdict: Highest Risk — 449 PS, high ownership costs

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve stem seals / turbo coolant lines
rows.ownershipVerdict: High Risk — same N63 issues, slightly cheaper

economicsRule.title: The F06 rule
economicsRule.text: A £4,500–£7,000 N57 recon against a £8,000–£25,000 F06 sits at 18–87% of the car's value. On a sub-£10,000 F06, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £20,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 22–35% — a repair decision, not a financial one. For an N63 650i or S63 M6, the economics are less favourable — value the car first, and always budget for N63-specific maintenance.

problems.title: N63 Valve Stem Seal Failure (650i)
problems.rootCause: The N63's hot-vee design (turbochargers located in the V of the engine) creates significant heat — valve stem seals can harden and fail, leading to oil consumption, blue smoke on startup, and eventually, catalytic converter damage. This is the defining issue of the N63 across all BMW models.
problems.recommendation: On any N63-powered F06, have the valve stem seals inspected at 50,000 miles — a £3,000–£6,000 preventative repair is dramatically cheaper than the £6,000–£10,000 engine replacement that follows if ignored. This is the defining ownership cost of the 650i.

cta.label: Compare N63 engine prices for your F06 650i →

problems.title: S63 Valve Stem Seal & Turbocharger Wear (M6)
problems.rootCause: The S63 shares the N63's hot-vee architecture but with higher outputs — valve stem seal failure is common, and the turbochargers can suffer from wear due to the increased boost pressures. The 7-speed DCT transmission also requires specialist maintenance.
problems.recommendation: On any S63-powered M6, have the valve stem seals and turbochargers inspected at 50,000 miles — a £4,000–£7,000 preventative service is dramatically cheaper than the £8,000–£14,000 engine replacement that follows if ignored. The M6 is rewarding but requires serious maintenance commitment.

cta.label: Compare S63 engine prices for your F06 M6 →

problems.title: N57D30B Timing Chain & DPF Issues (640d)
problems.rootCause: The N57 is generally robust, but the timing chain can wear on higher-mileage examples. Like all modern diesels, the DPF can clog with urban use if regeneration cycles cannot complete. The N57 is significantly more reliable than the N47, but these are the issues to watch for.
problems.recommendation: On any N57-powered F06, have the timing chain inspected at 80,000 miles and ensure regular motorway runs to allow DPF regeneration. An £800–£1,500 preventative service is far cheaper than the £4,500–£7,000 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare N57 engine prices for your F06 640d →

problems.title: Air Suspension Compressor Failure (rear air suspension)
problems.affectedModels: F06 models with optional rear air suspension (self-levelling).
problems.rootCause: The rear air suspension compressor can fail with age — the compressor motor seals deteriorate, leading to longer pump times, overheating, and eventual failure. The car may sit low at the rear or fail to level.
problems.recommendation: Have the air suspension system leak-tested at every service on F06s over 60,000 miles — a £500–£900 compressor repair is cheap insurance against the £1,200–£1,800 full system overhaul that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare F06 air suspension repair quotes →

replacementCosts.h2: F06 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are F06 Gran Coupé (four-door fastback) only. F12 Convertible and F13 Coupé share most of these engines but are covered on their own Generation Pages. The 640i uses the N55B30A (320 PS). The 650i uses the N63B44B (449 PS), with early production using N63B44A (408 PS) and some markets receiving the N63B40A (408 PS, 4.0L). The 640d uses the N57D30B (313 PS). The M6 uses the S63B44B (560–575 PS). xDrive (all-wheel drive) was available as an option on most variants. The F06 Gran Coupé features a 4+1 seating arrangement with two full-size rear seats and one smaller rear middle seat.
